speaker: interviewer
text: Kurt Lewin. Tell us about this picture please, Mr. Lewin.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my father, Dr. Ezekiel Lewin, the last Chief Rabbi of the Jewish community of Lwow, taken in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had it with me all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had-- and this was taken in Lwow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my mother, Rachel Rice Lewin. And this is a picture taken already in the first phase of the German occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who took the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My impression is the picture was taken for some document, probably in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had it with me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my picture as a schoolboy in primary school-- Polish primary school-- and I wear the typical four-cornered hat, or cap, which students wore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was taken in 1932 or 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lwow. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of my youngest brother, Leon, born in 1936, shot on the street by [INAUDIBLE] in front of my mother in, probably, November 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lwow, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had it with me. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is the Jewish synagogue, German-Jewish synagogue in Katowice. It was the first rabbinical chair occupied my-- fa-- by my father. He was a rabbi of this community for about five years before moving on to Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember this synagogue at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was three years when I left the city. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a pic-- picture of my father, Dr. Ezekiel Lewin, the last Chief Rabbi of Lwow, taken in 1940 for a Soviet passport, which every inhabitant had to have. It shows how the man has aged within a relatively short period of time, a year and a half. This was a time when he was systematically interrogated by the [INAUDIBLE] and attempts were made to prompt him to leave his position as the Chief Rabbi of the city. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of Andrew Graff Sheptytsky, Archbishop of Lwow and Metropolitan of [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He headed the Greek Catholic church, which is a church of the Byzantine rite, however, in union with Rome. He was a man with enormous compassion and humanity, a friend of the Jews. He issued the pastoral letter protesting the killing, and alerting his flock not to participate actively or passively in the horror. The title of the pastoral letter is

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- what is the signature on that? Is it addressed to somebody?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's just his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got it after the war. And he also has organized and inspired people in his church to save over 200 Jews-- men, women, and children-- who were sheltered in the monasteries and other institutions of this church.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Clement Graff Sheptytsky, brother of the Metropolitan, and the General of the Studite Order in which I lived during the war. He actually was responsible for sheltering and administration of this activity of Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got it after the war. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of the monastery in which I lived in Carpathian mountains, on the borderline of Hungary. And these are the monks with whom I lived and their Superior.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you on that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I tried to avoid being photographed during that period. I could be recognized. The monastery, I lived there for a year. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A very isolated place. The monastery was burned by the Germans and Hungarians retreating before the Red Army. The monks were deported to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken by one of the visiting fathers who came there to spend a vacation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got it after the war from the monks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are monks of the community in the Carpathian mountains, Studite monks with whom I lived for a year. And this carriage is a means of transportation. It was pulled up the mountain by a little narrow gauge train, and we went down simply by the force of gravity, almost 60 kilometers to the main city.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are they carting up there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, whatever. Food or God knows what we were carting. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a diocesal priest working in the [INAUDIBLE] of the Metropolitan, Father Ivan Kotiw, K-O-T-- T like in Thomas, --I-W. He was the administrator of the sheltering of Jews, providing them with documents, and was responsible for arranging shelter for almost 200 souls. He was arrested by the [INAUDIBLE] in 1947, spent a number of years in labor camps, reappeared in Lithuania some time in the '70s, and again disappeared-- probably died in one of their labor camp-- camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was sent to me by friends of mine from Lviv, from Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Monks. This is a Studite, Father Johannes Peters, born in Westfalia, a German who joined the order in the '30s. He dedicated his life to this order, and to serving the Archbishop and his ideas. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He acted as a courier between the Metropolitan and the papal nuncio, or senigo in Berlin, from 1941 to 1942. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he also sheltered, in two or three factories which he organized, Jewish families in the cellar. He was arrested by the Gestapo, and shipped to Dachau, where he survived the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken after the war , because, under certain circumstances which I described in my book, I had to defend him in an ecclesiastical court. Because he was accused by German bishops, because he told the German bishops publicly what he said all his life. If they were decent people, they would be with him in Dachau. And if he would be a Nazi, he would be a bishop today, not on trial. A rather unusual man, to say the least.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the senior superior of the Studites Order, Father Mark, or [? Markostek. ?] I smuggled him out of Poland disguised as a Jew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was instrumental in saving me and many other Jews, because in the order, he was administrating-- administering the procurement of documents, shelter, and taking care of the individuals. He ended in Canada, and as he loved fishing, he served in British Columbia. And this is a picture taken during my last visit to British Columbia some time in the early '70s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my picture, taken shortly after the end of the war, in the uniform of a Polish army officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was Second Lieutenant, Roman Matkowski.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who took it? Who took the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken by somebody in the military unit, because they had their own photographer and publicity man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my picture, taken five years later. I was, at that time, a major in the Israeli army. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have a-- sort of a decoration given to the [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] individuals who participated in the defense of Jerusalem in '47, '48. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is the first official document issued by the Labor Department-- German Civilian Labor Department-- to register Jews for forced labor. It might be interesting to know that it starts, 'Der Jude,' which means, the Jew, and obviously the star of David. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is the same document, registration of a Jew in Rava-Ruska, by the Labor Department [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for identification purposes and temporary exclusion from liber-- from deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I had it with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had it with you. Oh, you had all these documents with you all along?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were in the archives of the Metropolitan, and before leaving Lwow, I picked them up and carried them with me to be able to identify myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an interesting document, issued by the German Civilian Administration. It's an identity document stating that I'm employed by the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the German firm collecting rags and scrap metal, and states at the end, officially, with a stamp, that I am to be excluded from the deportation. And the last date of this document is 31st of July, 1942. It was not prolonged. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an identity card with my picture in a cassock, identifying me as Roman Paul Mytka, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a monk of the Studite Order living in Luzki, which was one of the monasteries where I lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's the date on that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The issuance date is August 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an identity document signed by the chief rabbi of the Polish-- Polish army, David Kahane, who states that Matkowski Roman and Kurt Lewin are the same person, and stating that I am the son of Dr. Ezekiel Lewin, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Chief Rabbi of Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a very interesting document, and a historical document. It's an identity card issued by the Jewish agency, its office in [INAUDIBLE], which was a recognized by the British government. And on the reverse, you will find a visa permit to enter Palestine, or Eretz Yisrael.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in 1946?

speaker: interviewee
text: '46, yes, a week before our departure from-- [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] So the upper is the visa-- official visa. This is the dust cover and the book I've written while I was waiting for [INAUDIBLE] in Lido di Roma, near Rome. It was translated into Hebrew by Dov Sadan, and published by Am Oved in Israel in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is about my experiences during the war, concentrating on the Holocaust and on my life at the Studites and the extraordinary [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] saga of [? Metropolitan ?] Sheptytsky and his brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: A variety of reasons and controversy surrounding the memory of the Metropolitan prompted me to write this book three years ago, which describes the Holocaust, my life among the Studites, service in the Polish army, the survivors and their attempt to rebuild their lives wandering through Europe, my stay in Israel, an encounter with the United States, and my work in Asia. The chapters not dealing with the war were introduced to provide my credentials and indicate the objectivity and my ability to portray something objectively and precisely. As a result of this book, Clement Graff Sheptytsky were-- was recognized as righteous by Yad Vashem, and so was Father [? Markostek. ?] And through these two individuals, the Studite Order, which opened its doors to the persecuted Jews and saved them at the risk of their lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you, again, Mr. Lewin, for sharing your testimony with us, and wish you well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. And I appreciate very much the opportunity to testify. And I wanted to thank you for helping me to bring this story out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
